goal:
Thirty MORE premium brand-carousel decks (keys 130–159) of educational
**healthspan / longevity** content for the main Vibe Health TikTok. This is round
two: every angle here is DISTINCT from the round-one batch (keys 100–129) and the
two reference decks (70 healthspan-vs-lifespan, 71 VO2 max). Each deck takes a
unique angle, ends on the real app screenshots, and reads calm + classy (not
hypey). Format rules: **NO em dashes**, real-photo cover (top band) via
`cover_query`, no button.

novelty: 130-zone-2-cardio: Zone 2 has saturated health TikTok for two years; the angle here doesn't add a fresh frame, stat, or contrarian edge to earn attention over the dozens of identical decks.
131-strength-after-50: 'Lift twice a week, progressive overload, protect your independence' is standard fitness advice with no fresh angle or surprising detail.
132-omega-3-brain: 'Eat salmon twice a week for brain and heart health' is among the most-recycled wellness talking points; there's no fresh angle, contrarian frame, or unexpected entry point.
133-gut-microbiome: Fermented foods and 30-plants-a-week have been circulating for years — nothing here reframes the idea or adds a surprising angle.
134-cut-added-sugar: Hidden sugar is well-trodden territory; the aging frame adds a degree of freshness but the specific swaps (plain yogurt, water over soda) are textbook.
135-salt-blood-pressure: 'Salt hides in packaged food, not the shaker' is a known health-media talking point — solid framing but not a fresh angle.
136-vitamin-d-sun: Vitamin D + sun + blood test is one of the most-repeated health carousel formats — nothing here reframes it or adds a fresh angle.
137-magnesium: Magnesium-for-sleep is among the most recycled health carousel topics; there's no fresh hook, stat, or contrarian reframe to make this version stand out.
138-morning-light: Morning light for circadian rhythm is extremely well-worn territory with no fresh angle or surprising data point.
139-caffeine-timing: Caffeine half-life and sleep cutoffs are well-traveled territory; the execution is clean but the angle isn't fresh enough to feel like a discovery.
140-screens-before-bed: Screen-time-before-bed advice is among the most saturated topics in wellness — no fresh angle, stat, or counterintuitive twist to differentiate it.
141-power-of-naps: '20-minute nap, not too late' is textbook sleep advice that has circulated for years with no fresh angle here.
142-break-up-sitting: Movement snacks and 'break up your sitting' advice is heavily saturated; the deck doesn't introduce a fresh angle, stat, or counterintuitive hook to separate it from the crowd.
143-take-the-stairs: 'Take the stairs' is one of the most recycled pieces of health advice on the internet — the framing adds modest polish but not a fresh angle.
144-sauna-heat: Sauna-heart-health is well-trodden wellness content; the honest 'association not proof' framing adds a small fresh edge but doesn't rescue a familiar angle.
145-breathing-hrv: Box breathing and slow-exhale content is saturated on health TikTok — the HRV angle is underplayed and could have been the differentiating hook.
146-posture-spine: The 'posture is strength not willpower' angle has been circulating in fitness content for a couple of years and won't feel new to algorithm-heavy users.
147-core-back-pain: Core-protects-back is among the most recycled pieces of fitness advice on the internet; dead bugs and planks add nothing unexpected.
148-flexibility-mobility: 'Use it or lose it' and 'hips and shoulders' are solid fundamentals but not a fresh angle; this carousel exists in a thousand forms already.
149-eye-health: 20-20-20, sunglasses, eat your greens, get check-ups — this is the standard eye-health listicle they've already scrolled past.
150-recovery-rest-days: 'Rest is part of training' is one of the most-recycled messages in fitness content — the execution is solid but the angle itself is not fresh.

151-walking-after-meals: Post-meal walking for blood sugar is a well-circulated idea on health TikTok; the timing-beats-intensity angle adds some freshness but won't feel new to a frequent health-content consumer.
152-loaded-carries: Loaded carries are underrepresented in health carousels compared to steps or sleep, so this angle earns points despite familiar longevity framing.
153-protein-per-meal: Protein distribution is a well-worn fitness talking point and nothing here reframes it in a way the audience hasn't already scrolled past.
154-eat-the-rainbow: 'Eat the rainbow' is one of the most repeated nutrition tips on the internet; even the polyphenol framing doesn't rescue it from feeling recycled.

155-handful-of-nuts: 'Eat nuts, live longer' is one of the most-covered nutrition talking points on health TikTok — there's no fresh angle, counterintuitive stat, or reframe to make this version stand out.
156-cold-exposure: Cold exposure content is saturated and this deck takes no contrarian or surprising angle to differentiate from the hundreds of cold shower carousels already out there.
157-leafy-greens-brain: Leafy greens equal brain health is among the most repeated nutrition facts on the platform; nothing here reframes it with a fresh angle or surprising data point.
158-gratitude-wellbeing: Gratitude journals and 'mind is part of health' framing are among the most saturated angles in wellness content.
159-know-your-numbers: 'Track your habits,' 'one place beats five apps,' and 'you can't improve what you don't measure' are among the most recycled lines in health content.
predicted_performance: 130-zone-2-cardio: Strong saves from the already-converted Zone 2 crowd, but low viral ceiling — the hook won't pull in newcomers and the concept is no longer novel enough to drive shares.
131-strength-after-50: Audience mismatch will hurt saves; the people most likely to save this aren't the people the account is built to reach.
132-omega-3-brain: Solid saves from the genuinely health-curious but unlikely to drive shares or comments because nothing here surprises or challenges the viewer.
133-gut-microbiome: Solid save-bait on a proven topic but the lack of a contrarian hook or fresh angle keeps it from breaking out above the average gut-health post.
134-cut-added-sugar: Strong saves potential from the label-reading tip but the topic is saturated enough that shares will be average unless the cover visual is arresting.
135-salt-blood-pressure: Strong saves from the health-curious 30+ crowd but limited share/virality potential given the familiar topic and a younger core audience that doesn't feel the stakes yet.
136-vitamin-d-sun: Low novelty and a soft hook mean saves will be below average for the longevity niche — this wins no new ground against established health accounts.
137-magnesium: Generic topic plus a soft cover equals low save velocity — this needs either a sharper angle or a surprising fact to earn the share.
138-morning-light: Solid save bait for wellness audiences but the generic topic limits organic reach and share potential.
139-caffeine-timing: High saves from sleep-anxious viewers but limited share/comment velocity because the information is familiar — it won't spark debate or surprise.
140-screens-before-bed: Low novelty and a soft hook predict below-average saves/shares — this won't earn the 'I never knew that' save or the 'send this to a friend' share.
141-power-of-naps: Solid saves from nap-curious viewers but low share potential given the lack of a surprising hook or counterintuitive claim.
142-break-up-sitting: Solid saves potential from the practical tip, but low novelty and a soft cover limit virality — expect average-to-slightly-above engagement, not breakout numbers.
143-take-the-stairs: Save rate will be low because the advice isn't surprising or specific enough to bookmark — strong brand voice can't overcome a premise audiences feel they already know.
144-sauna-heat: Saves likely from the 'honest about limitations' crowd but shares will be limited because the hook doesn't provoke and the habit isn't universally accessible.
145-breathing-hrv: Strong saves potential from the actionable pattern, but the generic topic and soft hook will limit organic reach relative to a more provocative framing.
146-posture-spine: Strong save candidate for desk workers but the mid-tier novelty score limits share velocity; expect solid but unspectacular numbers.
147-core-back-pain: Low save-share ceiling — the audience has seen this deck a hundred times under different thumbnails, so there's little reason to bookmark it over an existing save.
148-flexibility-mobility: Save-worthy for older health enthusiasts but unlikely to spike shares among the core 20–35 audience without a hook that makes them feel seen right now.
149-eye-health: Soft hook plus generic content means low save motivation — nothing here feels like a secret worth bookmarking.
150-recovery-rest-days: Strong saves likely from the checklist slide on under-recovery signs, but the well-worn hook limits the share ceiling compared to a more contrarian or data-specific angle.
151-walking-after-meals: High save potential from the ritual framing and the concrete 10-minute cue; share rate moderate because the core idea is familiar.
152-loaded-carries: Save-worthy for fitness-curious users but the niche exercise name limits broad share potential versus a hook built around a universal pain point.
153-protein-per-meal: Save-worthy for protein-curious users but the soft hook and familiar angle limit the share ceiling compared to a more contrarian or surprising lead.
154-eat-the-rainbow: Low save/share potential — the advice is too familiar to trigger a 'I need this' save, and there's no tension or contrarian hook to drive shares.
155-handful-of-nuts: Saves are plausible given the actionable portion tip, but the generic topic and weak hook ceiling limits shares and reach relative to a more contrarian or surprising deck.
156-cold-exposure: Weak hook plus generic topic means low save/share rate — nothing here gives a viewer a reason to bookmark it over the dozen cold-shower posts they've already seen.
157-leafy-greens-brain: Solid saves from greens-curious users but low share potential given the familiar premise — expect average-to-slightly-below for a longevity carousel.
158-gratitude-wellbeing: Soft hook plus zero novelty means saves and shares will likely land well below average for the longevity niche.
159-know-your-numbers: No hook tension and zero novel insight means saves will be low — this gets scrolled past by the exact audience it targets.

hooks: The easiest pace might *add the most years*.
After 50, strength matters *more*, not less.
One nutrient your *heart and brain* both want.
A *diverse gut* is a sign of aging well.
Hidden sugar may *age you faster* than you think.
Most of your salt is *hiding in plain sight*.
A little morning sun *goes a long way*.
The quiet mineral behind *calm, steady energy*.
Morning light *sets your whole day*.
When you sip coffee *shapes your sleep*.
The last scroll *costs you sleep*.
A short nap can *reset your afternoon*.
Sitting still for hours has *its own cost*.
The stairs are a *free daily gym*.
A warm sauna may be *quietly good* for the heart.
Your breath is a *dial for calm*.
Good posture is *built, not willed*.
A strong core *protects your back*.
Stay able to *move freely* for life.
Protect the eyes that *carry you* through life.
Strength is built on your *rest days*.
A short walk *after meals* changes everything.
Carrying heavy things builds *age-proof strength*.
How you *spread* protein matters too.
Eat the rainbow, *age more slowly*.
A daily handful of nuts, *a longer life*.
A little cold, a little *resilience*.
One serving of greens, *a sharper brain*.
A grateful mind, *a healthier life*.
Know your numbers, *guide your habits*.

summary: Brand voice and clarity landed well (4.57 / 4.37) but novelty cratered at 2.23, dragging predicted performance to 2.63 — every topic was a recycled wellness talking point with no contrarian frame, surprising stat, or unexpected entry. Critically, all 30 decks failed the same three code-eval checks (has_cta, follows_json_schema, has_image_prompts), indicating a systemic schema-output failure in the generator, not isolated mistakes.
what_worked: Brand fit: calm, classy tone was consistent across all 30 decks
Clarity: information was well-structured and easy to follow
Honest epistemic framing (e.g. 'association not proof' on sauna) read as credible
what_underperformed: Novelty (2.23): every topic was a known wellness talking point with no fresh angle, contrarian reframe, or surprising data
Hook strength (3.03): hooks were agreeable and soft — none created genuine tension or challenged an assumption
Predicted performance (2.63): no viral ceiling because nothing surprised or challenged viewers enough to share
100% code-eval failure on has_cta, follows_json_schema, has_image_prompts — systemic schema compliance breakdown

strategies.title: Force a contrarian or counterintuitive frame on every topic
strategies.guidance: Before writing any deck, ask: 'what does the average viewer already believe about this topic, and how does this deck surprise or challenge that?' If the answer is 'it confirms what they already know,' reject the angle and find a subversive entry point — a counterintuitive stat, an unexpected mechanism, a common belief that's partially wrong, or a reframe that recontextualizes familiar advice. Example: instead of 'Zone 2 cardio is good for you,' try 'The pace serious runners ignore is the one that adds the most years.' Never lead with consensus.

strategies.title: Open with a hook that challenges an assumption, not one that states a benefit
strategies.guidance: Hooks like 'A short nap can reset your afternoon' or 'Eat the rainbow, age more slowly' are soft promises — they confirm what viewers hope is true. Rewrite every hook to create mild cognitive dissonance or a knowledge gap: something the viewer thinks they understand but now doubts. Test each hook against: 'would someone who already knows this topic stop scrolling?' If yes, keep it. If no, add a specific number, a counterintuitive qualifier, or a reframe that makes the claim feel slightly dangerous or unexpected.

strategies.title: Validate CTA, image prompts, and JSON schema before accepting any generated deck
strategies.guidance: All 30 decks in this batch failed has_cta, follows_json_schema, and has_image_prompts — a 100% failure rate indicating the generator is not reliably producing schema-compliant output. Before the next batch: (1) confirm the prompt explicitly requires a CTA slide pointing to the app screenshots, (2) confirm cover_query is populated on every deck, (3) run the code-eval checks on the first 2-3 generated decks as a smoke test before generating all 30, and (4) treat any missing field as a generation failure requiring a retry rather than a manual patch.

strategies.title: Explicitly blacklist the most-recycled topic frames when prompting
strategies.guidance: The following angles are so saturated they scored ≤2 on novelty and should be blocked or radically reframed in any future batch: 'eat the rainbow,' 'take the stairs,' 'magnesium for sleep,' 'screen time before bed,' 'core protects your back,' 'gratitude journaling,' 'cold exposure = resilience,' and 'eat more protein.' If a topic from this list must appear, require a specific and surprising sub-angle (a mechanism, a counterintuitive population finding, an underreported nuance) that the generic version of the advice omits. The topic name alone is not enough justification to include it.
